elements.sent-2411-001: We show that, up to small error, the analog category of a finite group records the size of its largest Sylow subgroup.
elements.sent-2411-002: We continue the probabilistic reimagining of the foundations of topological robotics \cite{Farber:TCMP begun simultaneously in \cite{KnudsenWeinberger:ACC and \cite{DranishnikovJauhari:DTCLSC , in which motion planning is conducted according to continuously varying probability measures on the relevant space of paths.}}}
elements.sent-2411-003: The resulting ``analog'' invariants---which bound their classical counterparts, the Lusternik--Schnirelmann category and topological complexity, from below---display surprisingly subtle behavior.
elements.sent-2411-004: For example, the analog category of an aspherical space with torsion-free fundamental group is equal to the cohomological dimension of that group \cite[Thm.
elements.sent-2411-005: Thus, in this case, analog category equals category.
elements.sent-2411-006: On the other hand, in the case of a finite fundamental group, the classical category is always infinite, while in our setting we have the following \cite[Thm.
elements.sent-2411-007: Provisionally, let us call $G$ $a$-special if $|G|=ap^s$ with $p$ a prime, $(a,p)=1$, and $p^s>a$ (thus, a $1$-special group is simply a $p$-group).
elements.sent-2411-009: If $G$ is not $a$-special for some $a\in \{1,2,3\}$, then the universal upper bound does not hold for $G$.
elements.sent-2411-010: If $G$ is $1$- or $2$-special, then the universal upper bound holds for $G$.\footnote{Unfortunately, we do not know whether the universal upper bound holds for $3$-special groups.}
elements.sent-2411-011: For any $N\geq0$, there is a group $G$ such that $\mathrm{acat (G)<|G|-N$.}
elements.sent-2411-012: In other words, the universal upper bound holds for $p$-groups and fails for almost all groups not of prime power order.
elements.sent-2411-013: For groups of the latter type, we show that, up to small error, the analog category is recording the size of the largest Sylow subgroup.
elements.sent-2411-014: Let $G$ be a finite group not of prime power order.
elements.sent-2411-015: If $P\leq G$ is a Sylow subgroup of maximal order, then \begin{equation}\min\left\{2, \textstyle{\frac{|N(P)|}{|P|}}\right\}\leq \frac{acat(G)+1}{|P|}\leq 3.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-016: In more prosaic terms, the lower bound is $1$ when the largest Sylow subgroup of $G$ is self-normalizing\footnote{As shown in \cite{GuralnickMalleNavarro:SNSG , the admission of a self-normalizing Sylow subgroup places strong constraints on a group.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Prior to our work here, the quantity $\mathrm{acat (BG)$ was almost completely unknown apart from the universal upper bound and a calculation for cyclic groups of prime order \cite{Dranishnikov:DTCG .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Strictly speaking, this last calculation was of an a priori different invariant, the distributional category; we show here that the two coincide for finite groups, a special case of \cite[Conj.. 1.2]{KnudsenWeinberger:ACC .}
elements.sent-2411-019: For any finite group $G$, the analog and distributional category of $G$ coincide.
elements.sent-2411-020: In fact, the same argument may be used to show that the analog and distributional versions of the $r$th sequential topological complexity $\mathrm{TC _r$ coincide for every $r$.}
elements.sent-2411-021: We use the following non-standard notational convention for topological simplices: \begin{equation}\Delta^{n-1}:=\left\{(t_1,\ldots, t_n)\in[0,1]^n:\sum_{i=1}^nt_i=1\right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-022: We work in Steenrod's convenient category of topological spaces \cite{Steenrod:CCTS ---see \cite[Appendix A]{KnudsenWeinberger:ACC for a summary of relevant facts about these spaces.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Topological spaces are implicitly convenient, as are limits, including products, and mapping spaces.
elements.sent-2411-024: Convenient colimits, when they exist, are the same as ordinary colimits.
elements.sent-2411-025: The adjective ``compact'' refers to the definition in terms of open covers.
elements.sent-2411-026: We write $BG$ for the classifying space of the (discrete) group $G$, i.e., the geometric realization of its nerve.
elements.sent-2411-027: We write $EG$ for the universal cover of $BG$ and $X^{hG =\Map^G(EG, X)$ for the space of homotopy fixed points of the $G$-space $X$.}
elements.section-2411-002: The analog category of a group
elements.sent-2411-028: The purpose of this section is to establish the following formula, which the reader may take as a definition of the analog category $\mathrm{acat (G)$.}
elements.sent-2411-030: We begin with a brief review of the invariants of \cite{KnudsenWeinberger:ACC , which are defined in terms of the set $\mathcal{P (X)$ of probability measures with finite support on the topological space $X$.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: We view $\mathcal{P (X)$ as a topological space with the quotient topology inherited from the various maps \begin{equation}X^n\times \Delta^{n-1} \longrightarrowP(X) (x,t) \mapsto \sum_{i=1}^n t_i \delta_{x_i}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-032: We write $\mathcal{P _n(X)\subseteq \mathcal{P (X)$ for the subspace of measures with support of cardinailty at most $n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Classically, spaces of probability measures are often topologized using the L\'{e vy--Prokhorov metric, which metrizes the topology of weak convergence when the background space $X$ is a separable metric space.}
elements.sent-2411-034: We direct the reader to Section \ref{section:comparison below for some comparisons between the two approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-035: The twin advantages of ours are its generality, as we do not even require the background space to be metrizable, and its excellent technical features, which are summarized in the following result.
elements.sent-2411-036: Given a map $f:X\to Y$, we may consider the space of probability measures on $X$ with fiberwise support over $Y$, namely \begin{equation}P(f)=\left\{\sum_{i=1}^nt_i\delta_{x_i}\inP(X) : f(x_1)=f(x_2)=\cdots=f(x_n)\right\},\end{equation} and we set $\mathcal{P _n(f)=\mathcal{P _n(X)\cap \mathcal{P (f)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Sending an element of $\mathcal{P (f)$ to the point in whose fiber it is supported defines a continuous map to $Y$.}
elements.sent-2411-038: The analog sectional category of the map $f:X\to Y$ is the least $n$ such that $\mathcal{P _{n+1 (f)\to Y$ admits a section.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: The analog category of the space $X$, denoted $\mathrm{acat (X)$, is the analog sectional category of the evaluation map $(X,x_0)^{([0,1],\{0\})}\to X$, where $x_0\in X$ is any basepoint.}
elements.sent-2411-040: As our interest here lies solely in the aspherical context, we permit ourselves the abusive abbreviation $\mathrm{acat (G)=\mathrm{acat (BG)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: For the proof of the proposition stated above, we require the following standard fact.
elements.sent-2411-042: For any $G$-space $X$, the homotopy fixed point space $X^{hG}$ is canonically weakly equivalent to the space of sections of the canonical map $EG\times_G X\to BG$.
elements.sent-2411-043: Consider the following commutative diagram of mapping spaces: \begin{equation}\xymatrix{ \Map^G(EG,EG\times X)\ar[r]\ar[d] \Map^G(EG,EG\times_G X)\ar[d]\ar@{=}[r]^-\sim \Map(BG, EG\times_GX)\ar[d] \Map^G(EG,EG)\ar[r] \Map^G(EG,BG)\ar@{=}[r]^-\sim \Map(BG,BG) }\end{equation} The section space in question is the fiber of the righthand vertical map over $\id_{BG}$, while $X^{hG}$ is the fiber of the lefthand vertical map over $\id_{EG}$.
elements.sent-2411-044: The claim follows after noting that the vertical maps are fibrations and the lefthand square a homotopy pullback.
elements.sent-2411-046: If $X$ is a contractible $G$-space with an equivariant map $X\to \Delta^G_n$, then $\mathrm{acat (G)\leq n$.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Since $X$ is contractible, an equivariant map $EG\to X$ exists, which is to say that $X^{hG}\neq \varnothing$.
elements.sent-2411-048: It follows that $(\Delta^G_n)^{hG}$ receives a map from a non-empty space, hence is itself non-empty.

elements.sent-2411-051: This section is concerned with the construction of certain contractible equivariant cell complexes, which, via Corollary \ref{cor:contractible and obstruction theory, will be the key to proving our main results.}

elements.sent-2411-054: As a matter of terminology, we say that a space is $p$-acyclic if its mod $p$ reduced homology vanishes.

elements.sent-2411-072: Let $G$ be a finite group and $p$ a prime dividing $|G|$.
elements.sent-2411-073: A subgroup $H\leq G$ is called a $p$-intersection if it is an intersection of $p$-Sylow subgroups.
elements.sent-2411-074: The \emph({$p$-Sylow) depth of the $p$-intersection $H$ is the largest $d$ for which there is a chain $H=H_d<H_{d-1 <\cdots <H_1=P$ of proper inclusions with $P$ a $p$-Sylow subgroup and each $H_i$ a $p$-intersection.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: The ($p$-Sylow) depth of a $p$-subgroup $H\leq G$ is the maximal depth of a $p$-intersection containing $H$.
elements.sent-2411-076: The ($p$-Sylow) depth of $G$, denoted $d_p(G)$, is the maximal depth of a $p$-intersection in $G$.
elements.sent-2411-077: We adopt the convention that $d_p(G)=0$ if and only if $(p,|G|)=1$.
